title: Apple to spend $30 billion on Broadcom chips as it boosts US sourcing
gist: Apple signed a $30 billion-plus, multi-year deal (through 2031) for U.S.-made Broadcom RF front-end components including FBAR filters, prompting a $1.5B Broadcom expansion in Fort Collins, Colorado to produce at least 15 billion chips—a supply-chain resilience and policy-aligned onshoring move.

insights.claim: Apple's $30 billion-plus chip-supply agreement with Broadcom runs through 2031 and centers on U.S.-made wireless and RF components, including FBAR filters supporting 5G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth and GPS connectivity.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Locks in multi-year RF revenue for Broadcom and derisks a critical component category for Apple; a durable revenue anchor to model.
insights.quote: the multi-year deal extends through 2031 and centers on U.S.-made wireless and radio-frequency components, including FBAR filters

insights.claim: Broadcom will invest about $1.5 billion to expand its Fort Collins, Colorado facility, with the program expected to produce at least 15 billion chips.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Concrete domestic capex and a 15B-chip volume target justify Broadcom's investment and signal onshoring of RF manufacturing.
insights.quote: Broadcom will invest about $1.5 billion to expand its Fort Collins, Colorado facility, with the program expected to produce at least 15 billion chips.

insights.claim: RF chips are a practical category for U.S. capacity expansion because they rely on specialized manufacturing processes and long-term qualification cycles, unlike Apple's most advanced application processors and memory which remain tied to global foundries.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Frames which chip categories are realistic onshoring targets—RF yes, leading-edge logic/memory no—useful for handicapping other reshoring deals.
insights.quote: RF chips are a practical category for U.S. capacity expansion because they rely on specialized manufacturing processes and long-term qualification cycles
insights.locator: The deal also reflects Apple’s broader strategy

insights.claim: Locking in supply through 2031 gives Apple greater component-availability control, reduces geopolitical and tariff-related risk, and supports closer collaboration with Broadcom on custom silicon.
insights.speaker: M
insights.so_what: Tariff and geopolitical hedging is now a first-order driver of long-dated supply contracts; expect similar de-risking deals across the supply chain.
insights.quote: reduces geopolitical and tariff-related risk, and supports closer collaboration with Broadcom on custom silicon requirements
